did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper evaluates DERM (Deep Ensemble for Recognition of Malignancy), a novel machine learning-based digital health technology (AIaMD) that uses deep learning techniques to assess dermoscopic images of skin lesions for the classification of melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, and other malignancies in a real-world post-deployment setting.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' and reports prospective real-world performance data from the deployment of the DERM AI tool in two NHS hospitals. It presents original experimental results, including metrics on sensitivity, specificity, and caseload reduction, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Real-world post-deployment performance of a novel machine learning-based digital health technology for skin lesion assessment and suggestions for post-market surveillance
brief_summary: This study evaluated the real-world post-deployment performance of DERM, a deep learning-based AIaMD for skin lesion assessment, across two UK NHS hospitals between July 2021 and October 2022. The prospective analysis included 14,500 cases and 8,571 lesions with confirmed outcomes, assessing versions A and B of the tool for detecting melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and basal cell carcinoma. DERM demonstrated very high sensitivity for melanoma (95.0–100.0%) and malignancy (96.0–100.0%), with specificity improving from 40.7–49.4% in version A to 70.1–73.4% in version B. The AI tool identified 15.0–31.0% of cases as eligible for discharge, thereby reducing the caseload for hospital specialists in the urgent referral pathways. The results indicate that DERM's performance aligns with pre-marketing sensitivity targets and supports its safe deployment in diverse real-world clinical settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary malignancy target; sensitivity for detecting melanoma was 95.0–100.0% across versions and sites.
Malignancy target; included in malignancy sensitivity calculations (96.0–100.0%) and sensitivity targets (≥95%).
Malignancy target; included in malignancy sensitivity calculations and sensitivity targets (≥90%).
Malignancy target; included in sensitivity targets (≥90%) and identified in Fitzpatrick V/VI subgroup (n=1).
Premalignant/malignancy target; included in sensitivity targets (≥90%) and identified in Fitzpatrick V/VI subgroup (n=1).
Premalignant lesion; mentioned as a histology/clinically confirmed entity in the 'refer or not' classification definition and one lesion was referred as Bowen's disease.
Premalignant/lesion class; output by DERM and included in 'refer or not' true positive definition; identified in Fitzpatrick V/VI subgroup (n=3).
Non-malignant class; specificity for benign lesions was 40.7–49.4% (DERM-vA) and 70.1–73.4% (DERM-vB). Six subcategories of benign lesions were grouped together.
Defined as not melanoma, SCC, or BCC; 19 rare skin cancers identified, including the specific subtypes listed.
Specific rare presentation; two lesions were labelled 'benign' by DERM-vA but were later identified as subdermal foci of melanoma during root cause analysis.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, intraepidermal carcinoma, actinic keratosis, atypical naevus, or benign) and a binary 'refer or not' management recommendation.
all_task_types.details: The model DERM outputs a suggested diagnosis corresponding to the highest risk possibility among discrete classes (melanoma, SCC, BCC, IEC, actinic keratosis, atypical naevus, or benign). It also provides a binary referral recommendation (discharge vs. refer). Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, NPV, and PPV for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: DERM Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) Dataset
datasets_used.description: Prospective real-world data collected from the commercial deployment of the DERM AI tool within skin cancer pathways at two UK NHS hospitals: University Hospitals Birmingham NHS Foundation Trust (UHB) and West Suffolk NHS Foundation Trust (WSFT). Data includes dermoscopic images captured via smartphone (iPhone 6S or 11) and DSLR cameras, clinical information, and final diagnoses (histology or dermatologist assessment).
classes_used: Rare Skin Cancers (e.g., trichilemmal carcinoma, dermal sarcoma, atypical fibroxanthoma, marginal zone lymphoma)

use.testing: Used for prospective real-world performance evaluation (post-deployment surveillance) of DERM versions A and B. The dataset served as the test set to assess sensitivity, specificity, NPV, and PPV against final diagnoses.
use.other: The case-level population (14,500 cases) was used to assess service performance (caseload reduction, discharge rates). The lesion-level population (8,571 lesions with confirmed outcomes) was used for algorithm performance metrics.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Number needed to biopsy, treat or refer
Skin cancer conversion rate among discharged cases

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty of performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, NPV, PPV, and number needed to biopsy/treat/refer) for DERM versions A and B across different hospital sites.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance metrics were reported with 95% confidence intervals to estimate uncertainty, but no statistical comparisons between groups were performed or reported.

UHB DERM-vA.notes: 1,393/5,209 cases (26.8%) labelled eligible for discharge. Second-read reviewer overturned 502/1,393 cases (36.0%), of which hospital dermatologist discharged 197/502 (39.2%). 11 skin cancers (2.0%) found among these cases.

WSFT DERM-vA.notes: 168/945 cases (17.8%) labelled eligible for discharge. Second read overturned 81/168 (48.2%) cases, of which hospital dermatologist discharged 9/81 (11.1%). One skin cancer (1.2%) found among these cases.

UHB DERM-vB.notes: 1,486/3,603 cases (41.2%) labelled eligible for discharge. Second read overturned 588/1,486 cases (39.6%), of which hospital dermatologist discharged 232/588 (39.5%). No skin cancers found (0% conversion).

WSFT DERM-vB.notes: 297/1,410 cases (25.4%) labelled eligible for discharge. Second read overturned 146/297 cases (49.2%), of which hospital dermatologist discharged 38/146 (26.0%). No skin cancers found (0% conversion).

architecture_used.main_model: DERM (Deep Ensemble for Recognition of Malignancy)

architecture_details.description: A deep learning-based artificial intelligence as a medical device (AIaMD) tool. The system uses a deep ensemble approach to assess dermoscopic images of skin lesions. It outputs a suggested diagnosis corresponding to the highest risk possibility among discrete classes (melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, intraepidermal carcinoma, actinic keratosis, atypical naevus, or benign) and a binary referral recommendation (discharge vs. refer).

classification_or_output_head.output_logic: The output regarding suggested diagnosis corresponds to the highest risk possibility rather than the most likely classification. If a lesion crossed the defined threshold for melanoma, DERM would output melanoma even if it was more likely to be another class (e.g., seborrhoeic keratosis).
classification_or_output_head.binary_recommendation: Refer or not (discharge from pathway vs. refer to hospital-based consultant dermatologist)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: DERM (Deep Ensemble for Recognition of Malignancy)
preprocessing_techniques: Quality control and eligibility filtering based on intended use criteria (Table 1)
Exclusion of lesions obscured by hair, tattoos, or scars
Exclusion of lesions larger than the dermoscopic lens (>15 mm)
Exclusion of subungual, mucosal, genital, or palmoplantar lesions
Exclusion of patients under 18 years of age
Exclusion of non-potentially malignant lesions (e.g., rashes, eczema, infectious diseases, lupus)
Exclusion of lesions requiring monitoring for treatment response
Exclusion of lesions requiring staging of disease
Exclusion of open ulcerated skin lesions
conclusions: DERM demonstrated very high sensitivity for detecting melanoma (95.0–100.0%) and malignancy (96.0–100.0%) in real-world post-deployment settings across two UK NHS hospitals.
Specificity for benign lesions improved significantly from 40.7–49.4% in version A to 70.1–73.4% in version B.
DERM identified 15.0–31.0% of cases as eligible for discharge, thereby reducing the caseload for hospital specialists in urgent referral pathways.
DERM's performance aligned with pre-marketing sensitivity targets and supports its safe deployment in diverse real-world clinical settings.
The study highlights the importance of post-market surveillance for AIaMDs, noting that algorithmic improvements (version B) can enhance performance in real-world use.
No skin cancers were found among cases discharged by DERM-vB, indicating high negative predictive value and safety for discharge decisions in the updated version.

The authors suggest that future iterations should address limitations such as exclusion rates for certain lesion types (e.g., mucosal, palmoplantar, subungual) and improve explainability through techniques like saliency maps.
important_remarks: Significant conflict of interest: Multiple authors are employees of Skin Analytics Ltd (the manufacturer of DERM), hold shares/options, or have received funding/reimbursement from the company; the funder was involved in study design, data collection, analysis, and manuscript preparation.

Limited representation of darker skin tones: Only 159 lesions were assessed in patients with Fitzpatrick skin types V and VI, with very few confirmed diagnoses (n=1 each for BCC, IEC, actinic keratosis, and atypical naevus), limiting generalizability to this demographic.
Exclusion criteria limit applicability: The tool excludes mucosal, palmoplantar, and subungual lesions, which are critical areas for melanoma detection in patients with darker skin types.

Potential selection bias: While the study claims high consecutiveness (~80% of referrals), the QUADAS-2 assessment notes that exclusion criteria prevent a perfectly consecutive series, introducing potential patient selection bias.

Lack of baseline operational data: The study could not determine changes in biopsy rates, non-melanoma skin cancer diagnoses, or discharge rates for non-malignant lesions because robust baseline data prior to DERM deployment was unavailable.

No skin cancer conversion in discharged cases for DERM-vB: Although no skin cancers were found among cases discharged by DERM-vB, this was based on a second-read review overturning ~40% of discharge decisions, suggesting the initial discharge rate may have been overly optimistic without human oversight.
Performance metrics rely on 95% Confidence Intervals only: The paper does not report p-values or formal hypothesis testing for statistical comparisons between groups or versions.

Root cause analysis identified false negatives: Two subdermal foci of melanoma were initially labeled 'benign' by DERM-vA but were identified during root cause analysis, highlighting the risk of missing lesions without cutaneous changes.
